source.title: The role of orexinergic system in the regulation of cataplexy.

confidence_rationale: Recent (2023) single-author review consolidating the multi-target orexinergic gain framework: orexin modulates monoaminergic/cholinergic systems, brainstem REM-atonia generators, and limbic emotion-processing circuits. Directly relevant framing for MECH-281's claim that orexin gates coupling at multiple downstream sites. Confidence is moderate because it is a review and a single-author one rather than primary data.
failure_signatures: If orexin's anticataplectic effect can be fully reproduced by manipulation at a single downstream site (e.g., amygdala alone), MECH-281's multi-target framing is over-specified.

The Koyama review notes that the relative contribution of monoaminergic vs cholinergic vs limbic targets is still debated -- if future work cleanly dissociates them, the architectural commitment to BLA/CeA arbitration as the canonical motor-coupling target may need refinement.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Orexin/hypocretin prevents cataplexy by modulating activity at multiple points in a distributed cataplexy-inducing circuit, including monoaminergic and cholinergic neuromodulator systems, brainstem REM-atonia generators (vlPAG, LPT, sublaterodorsal nucleus), and emotion-processing limbic systems (amygdala, nucleus accumbens, medial prefrontal cortex).
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-281 names BLA/CeA, PFC (SD-033a), and the beta-gate (MECH-090) as downstream coupling targets of the orexin-analog gain. Koyama's review frames orexin as a multi-target gain modulator that acts at monoaminergic/cholinergic, brainstem-atonia, and limbic levels simultaneously -- consistent with the broadcast-override architecture SD-037 commits to. The mPFC inclusion in Koyama's circuit map is direct support for MECH-281's PFC coupling target. The review also confirms emotional triggers route through the limbic system rather than the brainstem alone, which is the dissociation MECH-281 implicitly requires.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Koyama is a review, not new data, and a single-author one. The mapping to the SD-037 broadcast-override architecture is good but inferential -- Koyama does not commit to a specific computational scheme. Treat as supportive context that the multi-target framing is biologically plausible.
mapping.source_context: Review of rodent narcolepsy/cataplexy literature, drawing on monoaminergic, cholinergic, brainstem-atonia, and limbic findings.

confidence_components.notes: Peptides is a legitimate but mid-tier specialty journal; single-author review. Mapping is high because the multi-target framing matches MECH-281 / SD-037 architecture; source quality is the limiting factor.
